01_initial_request.input_data: Systems biology research data across biological scales
01_initial_request.user_intent: Highlight the importance of sharing data for systems biology approaches to be effective and impactful
01_initial_request.quantifiable_goal: Efficient data sharing with deserved recognition and citations

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Systems biology research spans biological scales and requires collaborative data integration. [Gap] Sharing data effectively is challenging and requires alignment between partners and organizations. [Decision] To achieve efficient data sharing, chose to use FAIR Principles to perform data management on research data, expecting to obtain findable, accessible, interoperable, and reusable data.

parameters.reusable: enough provenance and information about prior use

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Data becomes sharable, comparable, and reproducible, enabling integration across diverse data types.

references.citation: Wilkinson, M.D.; Dumontier, M.; Aalbersberg, I.J.J.; et al. The FAIR Guiding Principles for Scientific Data Management and Stewardship. Sci Data 2016, 3, 160018.
references.relevance: Defines the FAIR principles used as the foundation for data sharing.

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] FAIR data is the foundation, but tracking contributions is necessary for recognition. [Gap] Data producers need credit to incentivize sharing. [Decision] To ensure deserved recognition, chose to use Persistent Identifiers (PIDs) to perform attribution on research products, expecting to obtain a citation chain linking data to publications.
02_agent_trajectory.observation: Enables tracking of data usage and ensures data producers receive citations.

references.citation: Wood-Charlson, E.M.; Crockett, Z.; Erdmann, C.; et al. Ten Simple Rules for Getting and Giving Credit for Data. PLoS Comput. Biol. 2022, 18, e1010476.
references.relevance: Provides rules for assigning credit and using PIDs for data.

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Data integration requires standardized metadata to ensure comparability. [Gap] Standards are often domain-specific and not explicit. [Decision] To make data comparable and reproducible, chose to use Community Standards and Infrastructures to perform metadata standardization on data reporting, expecting to obtain readable and comprehensible data for humans and machines.

parameters.regions: Elixir Europe, Australia Biocommons, H3ABioNet, NIH Office of Data Science Strategy
parameters.initiatives: GOFAIR, OpenAIRE, CODATA, Research Data Alliance (RDA)

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Data is described by standardized information, reducing concern about misinterpretation.

references.citation: Tenopir, C.; Rice, N.M.; Allard, S.; et al. Data Sharing, Management, Use, and Reuse: Practices and Perceptions of Scientists Worldwide. PLoS ONE 2020, 15, e0229003.
references.relevance: Highlights researcher concerns about misinterpretation and confidence in standardized data.

references.citation: ELIXIR, Australian BioCommons, H3ABioNet, NIH, RDA, GOFAIR, OpenAIRE, CODATA
references.relevance: Lists organizations providing training and standards for data infrastructure.

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Researchers value citation of their data. [Gap] Data sharing mandates are increasing but adherence varies. [Decision] To achieve recognition and compliance, chose to use Citation Practices to perform data crediting in manuscripts, expecting to obtain proper citation in manuscripts and compliance with mandates.

parameters.actionable_components: assigning FAIR data persistent identifiers (typically DOIs), including the DOI in the data availability statement and the manuscript reference section
parameters.mandates: Australia NHMRC (20 September 2022), NIH (25 January 2023), US White House OSTP (31 December 2025)

02_agent_trajectory.observation: 92.1% of researchers felt that having their data cited was important; mandates ensure data sharing.

references.citation: Tenopir, C.; Rice, N.M.; Allard, S.; et al. Data Sharing, Management, Use, and Reuse: Practices and Perceptions of Scientists Worldwide. PLoS ONE 2020, 15, e0229003.
references.relevance: Reports that 92.1% of researchers value data citation.

references.citation: Wood-Charlson, E.M.; Crockett, Z.; Erdmann, C.; et al. Ten Simple Rules for Getting and Giving Credit for Data. PLoS Comput. Biol. 2022, 18, e1010476.
references.relevance: Provides rules for linking diverse data across publications.

researcher_citation_importance.interpretation: Researchers felt that having their data cited was important

misinterpretation_concern.interpretation: Researchers expressed concern about the misinterpretation of data or data being used in ways it was not originally intended

03_success_verification.final_verdict: The commentary successfully highlights the importance of sharing data for systems biology by outlining FAIR principles, persistent identifiers, and citation practices as essential for effective and impactful research, supported by survey data indicating strong researcher consensus on the value of data credit and standardization.
